question.text: Can the State Bar of California hold public meetings that require attendees to sign in?

fact_001.content: The State Bar of California is expressly included as a “state body” for purposes of this open-meeting article (operative April 1, 2016).

fact_002.content: All meetings of a state body must be open and public, and all persons must be permitted to attend any meeting except as otherwise provided in the article.

fact_003.content: A state body may not require any person, as a condition of attending a meeting, to register their name, provide other information, complete a questionnaire, or fulfill any other condition precedent to attendance. However, an attendance list/register/questionnaire may be posted or circulated only if it clearly states participation is voluntary and that all persons may attend regardless of whether they sign/register/complete it. For teleconferenced meetings using an internet website/online platform that requires information to log in, the attendee must be permitted to use a pseudonym or other anonymous information.

question_002.content: What characteristics are "listed or defined" in Section 11135 (as incorporated by Section 11131’s nondiscrimination rule on facility admittance)?

answer_001.content: Section 11131 references “any characteristic listed or defined in Section 11135” but does not itself list those characteristics; the specifics depend on Section 11135.

working_answer.text:
No—if attendance is conditioned on signing in, the State Bar of California generally cannot do that for its public meetings.

- The State Bar of California is expressly treated as a “state body” for purposes of the open-meeting requirements. (Section 11121)  
- As a state body, its meetings must be “open and public,” and “all persons” must be permitted to attend unless a specific exception applies. (Section 11123)  
- A state body **may not require**, as a condition of attending a meeting, that a person **register their name**, provide other information, complete a questionnaire, or satisfy any other condition precedent to attendance. (Section 11124)  

### What is allowed
- The State Bar may **post or circulate** an attendance list/sign-in sheet **only if** it clearly states that participation is **voluntary** and that **everyone may attend whether or not they sign/complete it**. (Section 11124)  
- For teleconferenced/online meetings where the platform requires information to log in, the attendee must be allowed to use a **pseudonym or other anonymous information**. (Section 11124)

### Limits/uncertainties based on the provided text
- The materials provided do not include any exceptions that might allow mandatory sign-in in particular circumstances; Section 11123 notes exceptions may exist “as otherwise provided in the article,” but no specific exception language is included here. (Section 11123)
